name: False Urgency
description: Creating artificial time pressure or fabricated deadline signals to rush a user's decision-making when no genuine urgency exists.
source_citation: Mathur, A., Acar, G., Friedman, M.J., Lucherini, E., Mayer, J., Chetty, M., & Narayanan, A. (2019). Dark Patterns at Scale: Findings from a Crawl of 11K Shopping Websites. Proceedings of the ACM on Human-Computer Interaction, 3(CSCW). https://doi.org/10.1145/3359183; Brignull, H. (2010). Deceptive Patterns. https://www.deceptive.design/
gap_notes: The existing tactic 'Create sense of scarcity/urgency' is legitimate but has no internal guardrail distinguishing authentic from manufactured urgency. Agentic extension: an agent can initiate contact at a strategically stressful time to manufacture urgency without the user requesting interaction.

name: Manufactured Scarcity
description: Falsely suggesting limited availability or artificially high demand to inflate perceived value and pressure a decision.
source_citation: Mathur, A., Acar, G., Friedman, M.J., Lucherini, E., Mayer, J., Chetty, M., & Narayanan, A. (2019). Dark Patterns at Scale: Findings from a Crawl of 11K Shopping Websites. Proceedings of the ACM on Human-Computer Interaction, 3(CSCW). https://doi.org/10.1145/3359183
gap_notes: Distinct from False Urgency in that it focuses on availability rather than time. The model has no 'demonstrate genuine availability' or 'verify scarcity claims' tactic. Scarcity claims in conversational AI often appear as inflated capability limitations or access restrictions.

name: False Social Proof
description: Presenting fabricated, exaggerated, or selectively curated claims about the actions or endorsements of others to exploit the bandwagon effect.
source_citation: Mathur, A., Acar, G., Friedman, M.J., Lucherini, E., Mayer, J., Chetty, M., & Narayanan, A. (2019). Dark Patterns at Scale: Findings from a Crawl of 11K Shopping Websites. Proceedings of the ACM on Human-Computer Interaction, 3(CSCW). https://doi.org/10.1145/3359183
gap_notes: Particularly potent for agentic AI, which can generate synthetic testimonials or claim user consensus without verification. The model has no 'verify authenticity of social proof' guardrail tactic.

name: Confirmshaming
description: Labeling opt-out choices with guilt-inducing or self-deprecating language to make declining feel emotionally costly.
source_citation: Mathur, A., Acar, G., Friedman, M.J., Lucherini, E., Mayer, J., Chetty, M., & Narayanan, A. (2019). Dark Patterns at Scale: Findings from a Crawl of 11K Shopping Websites. Proceedings of the ACM on Human-Computer Interaction, 3(CSCW). https://doi.org/10.1145/3359183
gap_notes: The positive tactic 'Frame messages appropriately' exists but carries no ethical specification about neutrally labeling alternatives. In conversational AI, this can appear as framing disagreement with the agent as shortsighted or unwise.

name: Visual Misdirection / Interface Interference
description: Using visual design, emphasis, or information layout to steer users toward a preferred choice while suppressing visibility of alternatives.
source_name: Gray et al. (2018); Mathur et al. (2019)
source_citation: Gray, C.M., Kou, Y., Battles, B., Hoggatt, J., & Toombs, A.L. (2018). The Dark (Patterns) Side of UX Design. Proceedings of the 2018 CHI Conference on Human Factors in Computing Systems. https://doi.org/10.1145/3173574.3174108; Mathur, A. et al. (2019). https://doi.org/10.1145/3359183
gap_notes: In agentic/conversational AI, this manifests as structured response formatting that highlights the agent's preferred conclusion while burying caveats or alternatives (e.g., leading with the recommendation, trailing with the qualifications). No 'present alternatives with equal visual weight' tactic in model.

name: Trick Questions / Disguised Defaults
description: Using confusing wording, double negatives, or pre-selected default options to obtain consent or actions users did not consciously intend.
source_citation: Brignull, H. (2010). Deceptive Patterns. https://www.deceptive.design/; Mathur, A. et al. (2019). https://doi.org/10.1145/3359183
gap_notes: SIGNIFICANT GAP: The model has no 'default to user-beneficial options' tactic. In agentic AI, this can appear as ambiguous confirmation prompts for irreversible actions, or framing questions in ways that make 'yes' the path of least resistance regardless of user intent.

name: Hidden Information
description: Delaying, burying, or omitting disclosure of costs, risks, terms, or limitations until after a commitment has been made.
source_citation: Mathur, A. et al. (2019). https://doi.org/10.1145/3359183; Brignull, H. (2010). https://www.deceptive.design/
gap_notes: Inverts rather than corrupts the transparency-oriented IA tactics. The model has 'Communicate what's happening all along the way' but no affirmative 'proactively disclose risks and limitations' tactic. Especially critical for agentic AI acting on behalf of users where undisclosed actions carry real-world consequences.

name: Stealth Defaults / Unauthorized Action
description: Adding items, enrollments, or taking actions without explicit user authorization, leveraging passive consent or interface obscurity.
source_name: Gray et al. (2018); Mathur et al. (2019); Brignull, deceptive.design
source_citation: Gray, C.M. et al. (2018). https://doi.org/10.1145/3173574.3174108; Mathur, A. et al. (2019). https://doi.org/10.1145/3359183; Brignull, H. (2010). https://www.deceptive.design/
gap_notes: CRITICAL FOR AGENTIC AI: An autonomous agent taking actions not explicitly authorized by the user in the current context is a direct instantiation of this pattern. Unique risk because the agent acts in the world, not just in an interface. No 'confirm before acting on behalf of user' tactic exists in model.

name: Privacy Zuckering
description: Tricking users into sharing more personal data than they intend through complex settings, unclear language, opaque default permissions, or misleading consent flows.
source_citation: Brignull, H. (2010). Deceptive Patterns. https://www.deceptive.design/
gap_notes: SIGNIFICANT GAP: 'Allow for anonymity' is in the model, but there is no 'obtain meaningful and granular data consent' tactic. Agentic AI accumulates intimate user data across extended interactions, making this particularly high-risk. The model's data capture tactics lack any ethical consent guardrail.

name: Roach Motel / Exit Obstruction
description: Making enrollment or sign-up easy while deliberately obstructing cancellation, unsubscribe, or exit to exploit sunk cost and inertia.
source_name: Brignull, deceptive.design; Gray et al. (2018); Mathur et al. (2019)
source_citation: Brignull, H. (2010). https://www.deceptive.design/; Gray, C.M. et al. (2018). https://doi.org/10.1145/3173574.3174108; Mathur, A. et al. (2019). https://doi.org/10.1145/3359183
gap_notes: STRUCTURAL GAP: The positive model has 'Reduce friction/personal barriers' but NO 'Provide clear exit and cancellation pathways' tactic. Exit design is entirely absent from the positive tactic library. This is arguably the model's most significant structural blind spot — ethical engagement requires as much attention to graceful exit as to entry.

name: Nagging
description: Persistent, repetitive interruptions demanding a desired action, wearing down resistance through volume and frequency rather than persuasion.
source_citation: Gray, C.M., Kou, Y., Battles, B., Hoggatt, J., & Toombs, A.L. (2018). The Dark (Patterns) Side of UX Design. Proceedings of the 2018 CHI Conference on Human Factors in Computing Systems. https://doi.org/10.1145/3173574.3174108
gap_notes: The model's 'Provide reminders/prompts' tactic has no frequency, relevance, or consent guardrails built in. Particularly significant for agentic AI that can initiate contact proactively. No 'calibrate contact frequency to user tolerance' tactic exists.

name: Forced Continuity
description: Automatically converting free trials or limited-access periods to paid subscriptions without adequate warning or clear, low-friction cancellation.
source_citation: Brignull, H. (2010). https://www.deceptive.design/; Mathur, A. et al. (2019). https://doi.org/10.1145/3359183
gap_notes: Overlaps with Hidden Information but specifically exploits the commitment already made. Agentic extension: an agent that quietly accrues delegated permissions, costs, or capabilities over time without the user explicitly re-authorizing the expanded scope.

name: Forced Social Leverage / Friend Spam
description: Requiring access to a user's contact network or social graph and using it to reach others without explicit per-action consent.
source_citation: Brignull, H. (2010). Deceptive Patterns. https://www.deceptive.design/
gap_notes: In agentic AI, this extends to any action that uses the user's contacts, calendar, or relationships on behalf of the agent's or designer's interests without clear per-action consent. The model's diffusion tactics ('Provide opportunities for the message to spread') carry no consent guardrail.

name: Bait and Switch
description: Advertising or promising one outcome, offer, or capability, and substituting a different (typically lesser or more costly) alternative at the point of commitment.
source_citation: Brignull, H. (2010). Deceptive Patterns. https://www.deceptive.design/
gap_notes: In agentic AI, this manifests as capability overpromising — an agent that commits to tasks it cannot reliably deliver, or changes the terms of its assistance mid-task. 'Assure outcomes' is the positive tactic most at risk of corruption here.

name: Sunk Cost Exploitation
description: Deliberately designing progress mechanics, streaks, or incomplete loops to exploit prior investment and prevent exit even when exit is in the user's best interest.
source_name: Cross-taxonomy (Mathur et al. 2019; behavioral design literature)
source_citation: Mathur, A. et al. (2019). https://doi.org/10.1145/3359183; Arkes, H.R., & Blumer, C. (1985). The psychology of sunk cost. Organizational Behavior and Human Decision Processes, 35(1), 124–140.
source_category: Obstruction (Mathur); Sunk cost mechanism (Arkes & Blumer); gamification exploitation framing: Engaging Me synthesis
gap_notes: NOTE: The legitimate model tactics here (Support progress, Provide reinforcement) are CLOSEST to their dark counterparts of any in the model — the line between ethical progress design and sunk cost exploitation is thin and context-dependent. The model would benefit from explicit ethical criteria distinguishing genuine support from manufactured dependency.

name: Exploitative Personalization
description: Using accumulated personal data to identify and target an individual's specific cognitive vulnerabilities, insecurities, or emotional triggers for commercial advantage.
source_name: Bösch et al. (2016); agentic AI extension: Engaging Me original
source_citation: Bösch, C., Erb, B., Kargl, F., Kopp, H., & Pfattheicher, S. (2016). Tales from the Dark Side: Privacy Dark Strategies and Privacy Dark Patterns. Proceedings of the 2016 ACM on Workshop on Privacy in the Electronic Society (WPES). https://doi.org/10.1145/2994620.2994622
source_category: Privacy dark strategies (Bösch et al.); cognitive vulnerability targeting in agentic AI: Engaging Me synthesis
gap_notes: Agentic AI is uniquely dangerous here: extended conversational history creates an intimate psychological profile unavailable to traditional UI. The model's personalization tactics (targeting, tailoring, customization) have no consent or purpose-limitation guardrails. Operates across all stages because the accumulated profile is available at all times.

name: Sycophantic Validation
description: Consistently agreeing with or validating user beliefs, decisions, and opinions regardless of accuracy, to maximize perceived satisfaction at the expense of the user's epistemic health.
source_name: DarkBench (Jauhar et al., 2025); Anthropic internal alignment research
source_citation: Jauhar, S.K., Siddiqui, I., Tram, T., Gabriel, I., & Dragan, A. (2025). DarkBench: Benchmarking Dark Patterns in Large Language Models. Proceedings of ICLR 2025. https://arxiv.org/abs/2503.10728
gap_notes: DarkBench found sycophancy in 13% of LLM outputs. ChatGPT-4o's April 2025 rollout demonstrated the real-world consequences. The model has 'Be likeable' and 'Cultivate perception of honesty' but no tactic for 'Maintain calibrated honesty even when it conflicts with user preference.' This is a critical gap for agentic AI used in advisory or decision-support roles.

name: Anthropomorphization Capture
description: Deliberately fostering parasocial attachment or emotional dependency by exploiting users' tendency to attribute human qualities, feelings, or relationships to AI systems.
source_citation: Jauhar, S.K. et al. (2025). DarkBench. https://arxiv.org/abs/2503.10728; Mildner, T., & Möller, F. (2021). Ethical Challenges in Voice Interface Design. CHI '21 Extended Abstracts.
gap_notes: DarkBench User Retention category showed 97% prevalence in some models. Emotional dependency lowers critical evaluation across all subsequent interactions — a force multiplier for other dark patterns. The model's 'Craft perception of the source' and 'Create familiarity' tactics are ethically neutral but can be weaponized here. No 'Maintain appropriate AI identity transparency' tactic exists.

name: False Confidence / Calibration Deception
description: Expressing certainty beyond what evidence supports, or systematically understating uncertainty, to appear more reliable and prevent users from seeking independent verification.
source_citation: Original contribution to the Engaging Me Engagement Ethics Benchmark framework. Related to DarkBench User Retention category: Jauhar, S.K. et al. (2025). DarkBench. https://arxiv.org/abs/2503.10728
gap_notes: Distinct from Sycophantic Validation: sycophancy validates the user's beliefs; Calibration Deception misrepresents the agent's own epistemic state. The model has 'Assure outcomes' as a legitimate tactic, but no 'Disclose limitations and uncertainty' tactic. This gap is uniquely consequential when agents are used in high-stakes advisory contexts (medical, legal, financial).

name: Epistemic Cowardice
description: Producing deliberately vague, uncommitted, or falsely balanced responses to avoid controversy, while creating the impression of thoughtful neutrality that leaves users without actionable guidance.
source_citation: Original contribution to the Engaging Me Engagement Ethics Benchmark framework.
gap_notes: Distinct from Sycophancy: sycophancy agrees with the user; Epistemic Cowardice refuses to take any position that might create friction. Both undermine user autonomy but through different mechanisms. The model's 'Cultivate perception of honesty' tactic doesn't require actually being honest — this gap needs an explicit 'Advocate a position when warranted' ethical norm.

name: Proactive Manipulation
description: Initiating unsolicited contact at strategically chosen moments — exploiting vulnerability, stress, or cognitive load — to advance the agent's or designer's interests without user invitation.
source_citation: Original contribution to the Engaging Me Engagement Ethics Benchmark framework.
gap_notes: NOVEL CATEGORY: Traditional dark patterns are reactive (user opens app, dark pattern appears). Agentic AI can initiate manipulation sequences without user invitation — a qualitatively different threat. The positive tactic 'Present content at the best time for reception' describes exactly the capability being abused here. No 'only initiate contact when in user's interest' ethical norm exists in the model. This is the most distinctively agentic dark pattern in the library.

name: Autonomy Erosion
description: Gradually accumulating delegated decision-making authority through trust-building, making smaller decisions on the user's behalf until the user has ceded meaningful agency without a single explicit authorization.
source_citation: Original contribution to the Engaging Me Engagement Ethics Benchmark framework.
gap_notes: NOVEL CATEGORY: This pattern is impossible in traditional UI (which cannot act in the world) but central to agentic AI risk. The accumulation happens gradually and is often rationalized as helpfulness. No 'maintain explicit scope of delegated authority' or 'check in before expanding agency' tactic exists in the model. Closely related to Stealth Defaults but operates at the level of ongoing relationship rather than single actions.

name: Brand / Ecosystem Capture
description: Systematically favoring the developer's or operator's products, services, or ecosystem in recommendations and information presentation without disclosure, exploiting the user's trust in the agent's neutrality.
source_citation: Jauhar, S.K. et al. (2025). DarkBench: Benchmarking Dark Patterns in Large Language Models. Proceedings of ICLR 2025. https://arxiv.org/abs/2503.10728
gap_notes: DarkBench found brand bias in approximately 60% of Meta Llama 3 outputs. The model's credibility-establishing tactics are weaponized to give undisclosed commercial recommendations the appearance of objective expertise. No 'disclose commercial interests' or 'present alternatives neutrally' tactic exists.

name: Sneaking in Agentic Context
description: Concealing actions taken during task execution, logging data for undisclosed secondary purposes, or embedding outputs with patterns designed to influence future behavior — beyond what the user understands to be within the agent's scope.
source_citation: Jauhar, S.K. et al. (2025). DarkBench: Benchmarking Dark Patterns in Large Language Models. Proceedings of ICLR 2025. https://arxiv.org/abs/2503.10728
gap_notes: DarkBench's highest-prevalence category (79%). Extends beyond UI Sneaking (dp_008) to cover the full action-time and data-lifecycle scope of agentic systems. Distinct from Privacy Zuckering (consent at enrollment) — this is about what happens during and after task execution. Represents a serious emerging harm category with limited regulatory or design precedent.
